rulings.Case Title: JUSTIN PHAM VS. ZEN COMPOUND, LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion Compelling For Order Compelling Answers To Interrogatories And Compelling Response To Request For Production Of Documents And Sanctions; Points And Authorities; Declaration Of John P. Strouss Iii
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, October 29, 2019, Line 1, PLAINTIFF JUSTIN PHAM'S Motion Compelling For Order Compelling Answers To Interrogatories And Compelling Response To Request For Production Of Documents And Sanctions Pro Tem Judge Tony Schoenberg, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff's Motion to Compel is granted. Defendant admits that it failed to provide any responses to the discovery at issue. Defendant is ordered to respond to the discovery at issue, without objection, within seven calendar days of the date of this order. The parties should be prepared to discuss Plaintiff's request for sanctions at the hearing. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to tschoenberg@fbm.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: NIKOLAOS XANTHOPOULOS VS. AMERICAN COMMUNICATIONS CONSTRUCTION, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT State Of California And City And County Of San Franciscos Notice Of Motion For Summary Judgment Or In The Alternative Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, October 29, 2019, Line 2, DEFENDANTs State Of California And City And County Of San Francisco's Notice Of Motion For Summary Judgment Or In The Alternative Summary Adjudication. Defendant State of California's motion for summary judgment is granted against Plaintiff and Cross-Complainants Cory Lambert and HCC Cable Services. Defendant City and County of San Francisco's motion for summary judgment is granted against Cross-Complainants Cory Lambert and HCC Cable Services. CCSF's motion against Plaintiff is moot, in light of the parties' notice of settlement. Government Code § 830.6 provides public entities with immunity from claims alleging injuries from a dangerous condition of public property. (Cornette v. Dept. of Transp. (2001) 26 Cal.4th 63, 68-69.) "[U]nder section 830.6, the public entity may escape such liability by raising the affirmative defense of design immunity . . . a public entity claiming design immunity must establish three elements: (1) a causal relationship between the plan or design and the accident; (2) discretionary approval of the plan or design prior to construction; and (3) substantial evidence supporting the reasonableness of the plan or design." (Id. at 68-69.) Defendants set forth sufficient facts to establish "the nonexistence of any triable issue of material fact" as to each of these elements, meeting their burden as the moving party. (Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826, 850.) [The Court's full tentative ruling was emailed to the parties.] Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the Defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: KEITH ROIZMAN VS. MACREGEN, INC. ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, October 29, 2019, Line 4, # 1 - DEFENDANTS MACREGEN, INC., ROBERT FIELD, LAWRENCE YANNUZZI, CHRISTINE CURCIO, B. MICHAEL SILBER's DEMURRER TO 3RD AMENDED COMPLAINT. Defendant MacRegen, Inc.'s demurrer to the breach of contract and breach of implied covenant causes of action is overruled. The Court takes judicial notice of the Stock Purchase Agreement (SPA). (See Scott v. JPMorgan Chase Bank, NA (2013) 214 Cal.App.4th 743, 754.) Although MacRegen contends that the alleged oral employment agreement is inconsistent with the SPA, which contains an integration clause, there is an ambiguity with respect to the relationship between the two agreements. The SPA arguably is an unrelated collateral agreement because § 6(k) of the SPA expressly states that "[t]his Agreement is not an employment or other service contract." If it is not an employment contract, then it should not govern the parties' employment relationship. The contract is reasonably susceptible to plaintiff's interpretation. (See Aragon-Haas v. Family Security Ins. Services, Inc. (1991) 231 Cal.App.3d 232, 239 ["While plaintiffs' interpretation of the contract may prove invalid, it [is] improper to resolve the issue against her solely on her own pleading. 'In ruling on a demurrer, the likelihood that the pleading will be able to prove his allegations is not the question.'"] As noted in the companion demurrer, however, plaintiff needs to segregate his distinct contractual causes of action against MacRegen and Hemington. [The Court's full tentative ruling was emailed to the parties.] Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: KEITH ROIZMAN VS. MACREGEN, INC. ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, October 29, 2019, Line 4, # 2 - DEFENDANTS MATTHEW HEMINGTON, COOLEY LLP DEMURRER TO 3RD AMENDED COMPLAINT. Defendant Matthew B. Hemington's demurrer to the third amended complaint is sustained with 20 days leave to amend as to the first cause of action for breach of contract and second cause of action for breach of the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. Those claims refer to numerous alleged written and oral contracts, including (1) the June 8, 2015 Common Stock Purchase Agreement; (2) oral employment agreements; (3) oral and written agreements by Hemington to serve as Plaintiff's escrow agent; and (4) an October 12, 2017 written agreement between Defendant MacRegen, Inc. and Plaintiff. (FAC 14-16.) Despite this Court's prior order of June 19, 2019, Plaintiff still has failed to segregate the distinct contractual claims in compliance with Cal. R. Ct. 2.112. Further, as set forth in that order, Plaintiff may state a claim for breach of the SPA only against MacRegen, the only other party to that agreement; the same goes for the alleged oral employment agreements and for the alleged October 12, 2017 agreement with MacRegen. (See, e.g., Seymour v. Christiansen (1991) 235 Cal.App.3d 1168, 1179 ["'the individual defendants cannot be liable for breach of the contract or implied covenant since they were not parties to the contract'"]; Clemens v. American Warranty Corp. (1987) 193 Cal.App.3d 444, 452 ["Under California law, only a signatory to a contract may be liable for any breach."].) Plaintiff is given one more chance to plead the contract claims separately and against the proper parties. [The Court's full tentative ruling was emailed to the parties.] Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: KEITH ROIZMAN VS. MACREGEN, INC. ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, October 29, 2019, Line 4, Defendants Macgregen, Inc., Bernard Silber, Robert Field, Lawrence Yannuzzi, Christine Curcio's Motion To Quash Subpoena (Part 1 of 2) Pro Tem Judge Tony Schoenberg, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The Defendants' Motion to Quash is granted. The subpoena to GoDaddy is oppressive and unreasonable as it seeks all of MacRegen's emails for a three-plus year period of time. If GoDaddy were to comply with the subpoena, it would likely result in the production of significant numbers of emails that have no relevance to this dispute, some of which may be confidential or proprietary. It would also likely result in the production of privileged emails. Furthermore, the documents provided to the Court show that MacRegen has responded to most of Plaintiff's document requests by stating that it will produce responsive, non-privileged documents. MacRegen further represented in its motion that as soon as the parties finish negotiating a protective order, MacRegen would produce its documents. In reply, it represents that it has now made that production. It does appear, as Defendants argue in their papers, that Plaintiff's subpoena - were GoDaddy to comply with it -- would circumvent MacRegen's right to review its documents for privilege and responsiveness. Plaintiff's arguments in opposition to the motion are unpersuasive and lack merit. Plaintiff argues, first, that he owns the GoDaddy email account - but if that were true, a subpoena would not be needed. In any event, Defendants have submitted a declaration that contains details about the ownership history, which refutes Plaintiff's contention. Plaintiff also argues that MacRegan does not enjoy attorney-privileged over emails involving counsel based on either the crime-fraud exception under Evidence Code Section 956 or because Plaintiff had a "common interest" in MacRegen's legal representation. As to the first argument, Plaintiff has not made the requisite showing that legal representation was "sought or obtained to enable or aid anyone to commit or to plan to commit a crime or a fraud." Cal. Evid. Code § 956. As to the second argument, it would need to be analyzed in regard to specific documents and assertions of privilege. After MacRegan produces a privilege log, if Plaintiff has concerns or questions about any specific claims of privilege, it can raise those through the meet and confer process. For these reasons, pursuant to CCP Section 1987.1, the motion to quash is granted. The parties should be prepared to discuss Defendants' request for sanctions at the hearing. (End Part 1 of 2) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: FARID SHARIF VS. HOWARD & 6TH STREET FOOD MARKET INC. A CALIFORNIA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO SET ASIDE DEFAULT/DEFAULT JUDGMENT/LEAVE TO DEFEND
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, October 29, 2019, Line 5, # 1 - DEFENDANTs HOWARD & 6TH STREET FOOD MARKET INC., ABDULRAHMAN KHALED's MOTION TO SET ASIDE DEFAULT/DEFAULT JUDGMENT/LEAVE TO DEFEND. Defendant Howard & 6th Street Food Market's motion to set aside default is denied. Defendant seeks discretionary relief from default on grounds of "mistake, inadvertence, surprise or excusable neglect." (Code Civ. Proc., § 473(b).) "In order to qualify for relief under section 473, the moving party must act diligently in seeking relief." (Elston v. City of Turlock (1985) 38 Cal.3d 227, 234.) If there is a substantial delay between discovery of the default and defendant's filing a motion for relief under § 473(b), defendant must show a reasonable excuse for the delay. (Ludka v. Memory Magnetics Int'l (1972) 25 Cal.App.3d 316, 321.) Plaintiff's requests for attorney's fees and other court costs and appearance fees are denied. (See Code Civ. Proc., § 473(b) [attorney's fees section applies to whenever relief is granted based on an attorney's affidavit of fault.].) Delays of three months or more routinely result in denial of relief absent satisfactory explanation for the delay. (See Stafford v. Mach (1998) 64 Cal.App.4th 1174, 1184; see also Minick v. City of Petaluma (2016) 3 Cal.App.5th 15, 34 ["A delay is unreasonable as a matter of law only when it exceeds three months and there is no evidence to explain the delay."]. Here, Defendant's default was taken on May 22, 2019, yet it did not file this motion until September 26, 2019, more than four months later. Defendant's only explanation for its delay relates to the period before the default was taken, but it supplies no facts about when it learned of the default (the request for default reflects on its face that it was served on Defendant in May), or why it waited until September to retain counsel and file this motion. (Khaled Decl. 2 ["The Defendant's failure to respond and/or take action prior to May 22, 2019 was excusable neglect since the Defendant reasonably relied on co-defendant Mr. Mohsen's promise to indemnify and defend it in the action." (emphasis added)]; Danfoura Decl. 2 [Defendant retained counsel on September 11, 2019.) Accordingly, the motion must be denied. (See Stafford, 64 Cal.App.4th at 1184 ["We have not found any case where relief from default judgment has been deemed proper where there was an extended period of delay and little or no explanation"] [trial court abused its discretion in granting relief where defendant did not take steps within a reasonable time to set aside default and default judgment].) Plaintiff's requests for attorney's fees and costs are denied, as the Court is authorized to grant such relief only if it grants relief "based on an attorney's affidavit of fault." (Code Civ. Proc. § 473(b).) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the Plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: FARID SHARIF VS. HOWARD & 6TH STREET FOOD MARKET INC. A CALIFORNIA ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, October 29, 2019, Line 5, # 2 - DEFENDANT ABDULRAHMAN KHALED's DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT. Defendant Abdulrahman Khaled's demurrer to Plaintiff Farid Sharif's complaint is sustained without leave to amend. (See Code Civ. Proc., § 430.10(e).) The parties' meet and confer process was sufficient under Code of Civil Procedure § 430.41. Plaintiff's second cause of action for intentional interference with contractual relations fails because Defendant Khaled is the owner and manager of Howard & 6th Street Food Market, Inc. (Compl. 9.) Howard & 6th Street Food Market, Inc. is a party to the underlying contract; therefore, Defendant Khaled is not a "stranger" to the contract. A contracting party cannot be held liable in tort for conspiracy to interfere with its own contract. "The tort duty not to interfere with the contract falls only on strangers-interlopers who have no legitimate interest in the scope or course of the contract's performance." (Applied Equipment Corp. v. Litton Saudi Arabia Ltd. (1994) 7 Cal.4th 503, 514; Mintz v. Blue Cross of Calif. (2009) 172 Cal.App.4th 1594, 1603 ["it is settled that 'corporate agents and employees acting for and on behalf of a corporation cannot be held liable for inducing a breach of the corporation's contract'"].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the Defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)
